audienceSections.normal: This chapter teaches [[grammar:Dit is ...]]. It means [[grammar:This is ...]]. Use it to identify a thing nearby.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter introduces proximal demonstrative [[grammar:dit]] in the identificational copular frame [[grammar:Dit is N]]. Expert context: Dutch demonstrative agreement and de/het contrasts extend beyond this bounded construction and are not part of the Normal lesson.

audienceSections.normal: [[grammar:Dit is N]] means This is N. Put the thing you are identifying after [[grammar:is]].
audienceSections.expert: [[grammar:Dit is N]] is a demonstrative-subject copular clause with an identifying nominal complement. The interrogative support line [[grammar:Wat is dit?]] supplies discourse context without establishing a general wh-question system.

audienceSections.normal: Learn each new noun with [[grammar:de]] or [[grammar:het]], such as [[grammar:de pen]] and [[grammar:het boek]].
audienceSections.expert: The article-bearing citation forms preserve common-versus-neuter lexical classification. Indefinite [[grammar:een]] is introduced only in simple noun phrases here; broader article selection remains later material.

audienceSections.normal: These classroom examples are neutral everyday Dutch.
audienceSections.expert: The demonstrative identification frame is standard in spoken and written Dutch across the Netherlands and Belgium.

breakdown.normal:
- [[grammar:Wat is dit?]] asks what the nearby thing is.
- [[grammar:Dit is een boek.]] identifies a book.
- [[grammar:Dit is de pen.]] identifies the pen.
- [[grammar:Dit is het schrift.]] identifies the notebook.
- [[grammar:Dit is de school.]] identifies the school.
- [[grammar:Dit is het huis.]] identifies the house.
- [[grammar:Dit is de klas.]] identifies the classroom.
- [[grammar:Ja, dit is de klas.]] confirms the same identification.
breakdown.expert:
- [[grammar:Wat is dit?]] uses interrogative [[grammar:wat]] as the predicative complement of the copula.
- [[grammar:Dit is een boek.]] pairs proximal demonstrative subject [[grammar:dit]] with an indefinite nominal complement.
- [[grammar:Dit is de pen.]] pairs [[grammar:dit]] with a definite common-gender noun phrase.
- [[grammar:Dit is het schrift.]] uses a neuter article-bearing complement.
- [[grammar:Dit is de school.]] uses a common-gender definite complement.
- [[grammar:Dit is het huis.]] uses a neuter definite complement.
- [[grammar:Dit is de klas.]] uses a common-gender definite complement.
- [[grammar:Ja, dit is de klas.]] adds an affirmative response particle before the identificational clause.
